content.text: Root Management Group

Every Azure Active Directory (Entra ID) tenant has exactly one Root Management Group. It is created automatically when you first use management groups and cannot be deleted or moved. Its ID is always the same as the tenant ID. Every subscription and every child management group ultimately inherits from the root — making it the single point from which tenant-wide governance policies and role assignments can be applied.

attrs.alt: Azure Management Group hierarchy with root at the top

content.text: What the Root Management Group Is

The Root Management Group occupies the top of the management group tree. It has no parent. Unlike child management groups that you create and name, the root is created by the platform and its display name is 'Tenant Root Group' by default (though you can rename it). Its position in the hierarchy means that any Azure Policy or RBAC role assignment applied at the root scope flows down through every child management group and every subscription in the tenant.
Property
Value / Behaviour
Group ID
Same as the Azure AD / Entra ID tenant ID
Can be deleted
No — it is permanent for the lifetime of the tenant
Can be moved
No — it is always the root; it has no parent
Policies applied here
Inherited by all subscriptions and child MGs
RBAC assigned here
Inherited by all subscriptions, resource groups, and resources
Default access
Global Admins can elevate to User Access Administrator to gain access

Because the root management group is the ancestor of every resource in the tenant, use it sparingly. Assign only policies and roles that must apply universally — deny policies for truly prohibited actions (e.g. disabling diagnostic logs), and read-only roles for security tooling that needs tenant-wide visibility.
Elevating Access to the Root

By default, no one — not even Global Admins — has RBAC access to the root management group. A Global Admin must explicitly elevate their access to gain User Access Administrator at the root, and that access should be used, then revoked, following the principle of just-in-time access.
Elevate and then assign roles at root

# Step 1: Elevate Global Admin access to root (requires Global Admin role)
# This is a one-time operation done via the Azure portal:
# Azure Active Directory -> Properties -> Access management for Azure resources -> Yes

# Or via REST API:
az rest --method post \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/elevateAccess?api-version=2016-07-01'

# After elevation, the Global Admin has User Access Administrator at root MG scope
# Verify the assignment
az role assignment list \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<tenantId>' \
  --query '[].{Principal:principalName, Role:roleDefinitionName}' \
  -o table

# Step 2: Assign Owner to a dedicated governance admin (not the Global Admin account)
TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)
GOVERNANCE_ADMIN_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $GOVERNANCE_ADMIN_OID \
  --role Owner \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TENANT_ID"

# Step 3: Remove the elevated Global Admin access
MY_OID=$(az ad signed-in-user show --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment delete \
  --assignee $MY_OID \
  --role 'User Access Administrator' \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TENANT_ID"
Applying Policy at Root Scope

A policy or initiative assigned at the root management group applies to every resource in the tenant, including resources created in subscriptions that are added to the tenant in the future. This is the right level for truly universal requirements: mandatory diagnostic settings, allowed regions for all workloads, or deny rules for globally prohibited resource types.
Assign a built-in policy at root

TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)
ROOT_SCOPE="/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TENANT_ID"

# Find the built-in policy definition ID for 'Require a tag on resources'
POLICY_ID=$(az policy definition list --query \
  "[?displayName=='Require a tag on resources'].id" -o tsv | head -1)

# Assign it at the root scope with a tag parameter
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'require-env-tag' \
  --display-name 'Require environment tag on all resources' \
  --policy $POLICY_ID \
  --scope $ROOT_SCOPE \
  --params '{"tagName":{"value":"environment"}}'
What flows from root vs what should stay at root
Governance requirement
Apply at root?
Rationale
Require diagnostic logs on all resources
Yes
Universal compliance requirement across the entire tenant
Deny public IP creation
No — use Corp or Platform MG
May be legitimate in sandbox or DMZ subscriptions
Require 'environment' tag
Yes
All resources should be tagged for cost attribution
Restrict allowed VM SKUs
No — use workload-specific MG
SKU constraints differ between dev and production
Security Center auto-provisioning
Yes
Tenant-wide security monitoring
Contributor role for a specific team
No — assign at appropriate MG
Over-permissive at root; use narrowest scope
Hierarchy Depth and Limits

The management group tree can be up to six levels deep, not counting the root. In practice, three to four levels is sufficient for most organisations. The root is level 0; its direct children are level 1; grandchildren are level 2; and so on. Deeper hierarchies are harder to reason about and make policy inheritance more complex to audit.
Maximum depth: 6 levels below root (7 levels total including root)
Maximum management groups per tenant: 10,000
Maximum subscriptions per management group: no hard limit; practical limit is manageability
A subscription can belong to only one management group at a time
A management group can have multiple parent paths only through the root (which is the common ancestor)

# List the full management group hierarchy
az account management-group list --query '[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName, Parent:details.parent.id}' -o table

# Show the hierarchy as a tree structure
az account management-group show \
  --name $TENANT_ID \
  --expand \
  --recurse \
  --query 'children'
Creating and Organising Child Management Groups

The root's direct children are typically organised around the Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) landing zone model: a Platform MG for shared infrastructure (hub networking, identity, management), a Corp MG for regulated workloads, a Sandbox MG for experimentation, and optionally a Decomissioned MG for subscriptions being wound down. These are conventions — the structure should reflect your organisation's governance boundaries.

TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)

# Create CAF-aligned top-level management groups
az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-platform' \
  --display-name 'Platform' \
  --parent $TENANT_ID

az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-corp' \
  --display-name 'Corp' \
  --parent $TENANT_ID

az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-sandbox' \
  --display-name 'Sandbox' \
  --parent $TENANT_ID

az account management-group create \
  --name 'mg-decommissioned' \
  --display-name 'Decommissioned' \
  --parent $TENANT_ID

# Move a subscription into a management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name 'mg-sandbox' \
  --subscription $SANDBOX_SUB_ID
Terraform: Defining the Root and Child Hierarchy

Managing the management group hierarchy in Terraform lets you track structure changes in version control and apply the same hierarchy across multiple tenants (useful in multi-tenant MSP scenarios). The `azurerm_management_group` resource references the root by its tenant ID.
Auditing Root-Level Assignments

Because root-level assignments affect every resource in the tenant, it is important to audit them regularly. Any unexpected policy or RBAC assignment at root scope represents a potential security control gap or a misconfiguration with tenant-wide blast radius.

TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)
ROOT_SCOPE="/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TENANT_ID"

# List all RBAC assignments at root scope
az role assignment list \
  --scope $ROOT_SCOPE \
  --query '[].{Principal:principalName, Role:roleDefinitionName, PrincipalType:principalType}' \
  -o table

# List all policy assignments at root scope
az policy assignment list \
  --scope $ROOT_SCOPE \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Policy:policyDefinitionId, Effect:parameters.effect.value}' \
  -o table

// Azure Activity Log: changes to root management group assignments
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where ResourceProviderValue == 'MICROSOFT.MANAGEMENT'
| where OperationNameValue in (
    'MICROSOFT.AUTHORIZATION/ROLEASSIGNMENTS/WRITE',
    'MICROSOFT.AUTHORIZATION/POLICYASSIGNMENTS/WRITE'
  )
| where ResourceId contains '/managementGroups/<tenantId>'
| project TimeGenerated, OperationNameValue, Caller, Properties
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Deploying Root-Level Governance with Azure Bicep

Bicep provides a tenant-scoped deployment target (`targetScope = 'tenant'`) that lets you manage the root management group, create child groups, and apply root-level policies from a single template. Tenant-scoped deployments require Owner or Management Group Contributor at the root.

# Deploy a tenant-scoped Bicep template
# Requires: az account set --subscription <any sub in the tenant>

az deployment tenant create \
  --name 'mg-hierarchy-baseline' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file management-groups.bicep \
  --parameters sandboxSubscriptionId=$SANDBOX_SUB_ID

Tenant-scoped deployments are the recommended approach for automating the initial management group hierarchy. They run once and idempotently — re-running the same template converges to the same state without duplicating resources.
Moving Subscriptions Between Management Groups

When a subscription changes purpose (e.g., a sandbox subscription is promoted to production), you move it to a new management group. The move is immediate and the subscription immediately inherits the new parent's policies and role assignments. Any policies from the old parent are no longer applied.

# Move a subscription from Sandbox MG to Corp/Engineering MG
SUB_ID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# Remove from current parent
az account management-group subscription remove \
  --name 'mg-sandbox' \
  --subscription $SUB_ID

# Add to new parent
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name 'mg-corp-engineering' \
  --subscription $SUB_ID

# Verify the new parent
az account management-group show \
  --name 'mg-corp-engineering' \
  --expand \
  --query 'children[?name==`'$SUB_ID'`].name' \
  -o tsv
Policy inheritance changes immediately: after the move, policies from the new parent apply and old-parent policies are removed. Azure Policy compliance re-evaluation occurs within minutes.
RBAC assignments at the old parent's scope no longer apply to the subscription after the move. Principals with roles only at the old MG scope will lose access.
Resource configurations inside the subscription (VMs, storage accounts, etc.) are not affected by the move — only governance policies and inherited RBAC change.

If the subscription has resources that violate the new parent's deny policies, those resources are not deleted — they simply fall out of compliance. New resource creation that would violate the policies is blocked.
Delegated Administration with PIM at Root Scope

For emergency break-glass access to the root management group, use Privileged Identity Management (PIM) with eligible-only assignments. No one holds a permanent Owner role at root; instead, a small group of administrators can activate their role for a bounded time window with justification and MFA.

# Configure an eligible Owner assignment at root via PIM (Graph API)
# Requires PIM P2 license and Global Administrator or Privileged Role Administrator

TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)
ROOT_SCOPE="/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TENANT_ID"
ADMIN_OID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

ACCESS_TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token \
  --resource https://management.azure.com/ --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Create an eligible role assignment via PIM
curl -s -X PUT \
  "https://management.azure.com${ROOT_SCOPE}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleEligibilityScheduleRequests/${REQUEST_ID}?api-version=2022-04-01-preview" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "properties": {
      "roleDefinitionId": "${ROOT_SCOPE}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635",
      "principalId": "${ADMIN_OID}",
      "requestType": "AdminAssign",
      "scheduleInfo": {
        "startDateTime": null,
        "expiration": { "type": "NoExpiration" }
      },
      "justification": "Break-glass owner for root MG emergency access"
    }
  }'

With PIM eligible assignments, the Owner role is activated on demand for a maximum of 8 hours (configurable), requires MFA, records a justification, and generates an audit log entry. This satisfies both the principle of least privilege and break-glass emergency requirements simultaneously.
Common Mistakes with the Root Management Group
Mistake
Consequence
Correct approach
Keeping Global Admin elevated access to root permanently
Effectively a permanent tenant-wide Owner; violates least privilege
Elevate only when needed, assign to a dedicated account, revoke immediately after
Assigning broad RBAC (Contributor) at root for a monitoring team
Every subscription can be modified by the team, not just read
Use Reader at root; specific write permissions at narrower scopes
Applying restrictive deny policies at root without exceptions
Future subscriptions (sandbox, legacy) may be blocked from creating necessary resource types
Apply deny policies at Corp or Platform MG; leave Sandbox exempt
Not tracking the root MG in IaC
Ad-hoc changes to root drift from the intended state
Reference root in Terraform/Bicep; track child creation and policy assignments in source control
Using the tenant root as the only management group
No inheritance segmentation — all policies must be either universal or applied per-subscription
Create at least Platform / Corp / Sandbox top-level groups to enable policy differentiation
Checking Inherited Policies for a Subscription

When troubleshooting why a resource creation is denied or a compliance check is failing, it helps to list all policies that apply to a subscription — including those inherited from parent management groups up to the root.

SUB_ID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# List all policy assignments that apply to a subscription
# The --scope flag with a subscription ID returns inherited assignments too
az policy assignment list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Scope:scope, Policy:policyDefinitionId}' \
  -o table

# Check compliance state for a specific resource
RESOURCE_ID='/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/.../providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM'
az policy state list \
  --resource $RESOURCE_ID \
  --query '[].{Policy:policyDefinitionName, Compliant:isCompliant, Reason:complianceReasonCode}' \
  -o table

# Show policy assignment details including inheritance scope
az policy assignment show \
  --name 'require-env-tag' \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TENANT_ID"
Summary

The Root Management Group is the immutable top of the Azure governance hierarchy, automatically created with the same ID as the tenant. It is the only place where a policy or RBAC assignment can reach every subscription and resource in the tenant. Access to the root is intentionally restricted — Global Admins must explicitly elevate before making changes, and that elevated access should be revoked immediately after use. Structure your management group hierarchy beneath the root using a model aligned with governance boundaries (Platform, Corp, Sandbox), assign only truly universal policies at root scope, and audit root-level assignments regularly for unexpected changes.
